https://github.com/coreai-microsoft/caid: Engineering teams already want AI agents to handle testing, but preview-before-execute is non-negotiable for agent-generated tests. Cost, noise, and approval fatigue are the biggest adoption risks, while log-driven test suggestions, early release risk signals, and CI/CD integration nudges are the quick wins worth investing in.

All three power users independently architect their entire multi-agent workflows around context window limits — splitting sessions, clearing context between tasks, committing markdown plans to preserve state. The real problem: quality degrades silently before context runs out, and there is no signal telling developers when output has started to rot.

Replaced manual monthly AITK/Foundry extension survey analysis with an AI-powered Qualtrics agent that lets PMs, engineers, and designers chat directly with survey data to surface PMF, NPS, bugs, UX issues, and recommended customers on demand.

Ran a prioritization study and observation study on solution templates, identifying which templates matter most and where the experience breaks down — next steps include LLM-moderated navigation testing and syncing with marketing partners.
Internal 1P workshop reviewing an agent summary concept for responsible AI, bringing stakeholders together to stress-test the design before implementation.
Evaluated the federation of DSB (Data Safety Board) processes with customers, assessing how responsible AI review activities can be distributed and streamlined across teams.
UW students ran a usability study on adding local data sources in Foundry, revealing where the creation flow confuses users and where the experience needs polish before broader rollout.
